coredata.dc:title: One-step microwave preparation of carbon dots-composited G-quartet hydrogels with controllable chirality and circularly polarized luminescence
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Zhiwei
Chen, Jingqi
Yan, Xuetao
Liu, Xiaowei
Chen, Yingying
Zhao, Chuanqi
Feng, Lingyan
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) due to their excellent photostability, good biocompatibility and multicolor fluorescence, have been preferentially selected as luminescent fluorophores and co-assembled with various chiral host templates in recent years. The as-prepared composites exhibit marvellous circularly polarized luminescence (CPL) activity and show promising potentials in the fields of stereopsis and optoelectronic display, which have drawn increasing attention. However, the fussy assembly procedure and over-reliance on template chirality extremely limit the design and application of CDs-composited materials in the field of CPL. In this work, we directly prepared the CDs-composited G-quartet hydrogels with CPL activity using a one-step microwave-assisted method, and their helical chiral structure and CPL orientation can be arbitrarily switched back and forth between left-handed and right-handed through K+ and formation kinetics. Finally, the CDs-composited G-quartet hydrogels can be applied in anti-counterfeiting printing for encrypted information transfer, and their outstanding thermal cycling stability and CPL dissymmetry factor (glum) of up to 10−1 demonstrate a wider application potential in CPL active materials based on CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots (CDs)
G-quartet hydrogels
Circularly polarized luminescence (CPL)
Chirality regulation

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of the GMP-CDs (inset: particle size distribution histogram of GMP-CDs). (B) FL excitation and emission spectra of the GMP-CDs (inset: normalized FL emission spectra of GMP-CDs excited at various wavelengths); SEM images of (C) GMP-CDs-gel and (D) GMP-CDs/K+-gel.
(A) FTIR spectra and (B) CD spectra of GMP-CDs-gel and GMP-CDs/K+-gel; (C) CPL signal and (D) glum curve of GMP-CDs-gel with different K+ content (0, 1, 2, 4, 8, 12, 16, 20 mM).

CPL spectra (A) and melting curve (B) of GMP-CDs-gel and GMP-CDs/K+-gel under different temperature; Normalized CPL intensity of GMP-CDs-gel (C) and GMP-CDs/K+-gel (D) under cyclical thermal treatment.
CD spectra (A) and CPL spectra (B) of GMP-CDs/K+-gel under different vortex speeds; CPL spectra (C) and glum curve (D) of GMP-CDs/K+-gel under different annealing rate (Values are taken at 415 nm).
(A) Illustration of the potential of CDs-composited fluorescent G-quartet hydrogels for polarization-based encryption; (B) viewed under naked eyes and polarizing filters.
Illustration of the structures of CDs-composited fluorescent G-quartet hydrogels.

head.ce:title: One-step microwave preparation of carbon dots-composited G-quartet hydrogels with controllable chirality and circularly polarized luminescence

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Genome Institute and Shanghai Engineering Research Center of Organ Repair, Shanghai University, 99 Shangda Road, Shanghai, 200444, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Materials Genome Institute and Shanghai Engineering Research Center of Organ Repair

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Materials Genome Institute, Shanghai University, 99 Shangda Road, Shanghai 200444, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: QianWeichang College, Shanghai University, 99 Shangda Road, Shanghai, 200444, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: QianWeichang College, Shanghai University, 99 Shangda Road, Shanghai 200444, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Chemical Biology and State Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Resource Utilization, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Jilin, 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Chemical Biology and State Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Resource Utilization

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory of Chemical Biology and State Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Resource Utilization, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Jilin 130022, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Materials Genome Institute, Shanghai University, 99 Shangda Road, Shanghai, 200444, PR China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) due to their excellent photostability, good biocompatibility and multicolor fluorescence, have been preferentially selected as luminescent fluorophores and co-assembled with various chiral host templates in recent years. The as-prepared composites exhibit marvellous circularly polarized luminescence (CPL) activity and show promising potentials in the fields of stereopsis and optoelectronic display, which have drawn increasing attention. However, the fussy assembly procedure and over-reliance on template chirality extremely limit the design and application of CDs-composited materials in the field of CPL. In this work, we directly prepared the CDs-composited G-quartet hydrogels with CPL activity using a one-step microwave-assisted method, and their helical chiral structure and CPL orientation can be arbitrarily switched back and forth between left-handed and right-handed through K+ and formation kinetics. Finally, the CDs-composited G-quartet hydrogels can be applied in anti-counterfeiting printing for encrypted information transfer, and their outstanding thermal cycling stability and CPL dissymmetry factor (glum) of up to 10−1 demonstrate a wider application potential in CPL active materials based on CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: As an important branch of functionalization of chiral materials, circularly polarized luminescence (CPL) materials have attracted great interest and have broad application potential in the fields of optical recording, liquid crystal displays, optical sensing, catalysts for asymmetric synthesis due to their high directivity, spatial resolution, selectivity and sensitivity [1–5]. Excellent CPL-active materials are mainly judged by their CPL dissymmetry factor (glum) and fluorescence quantum yield (QY). According to glum = 2 (IL − IR)/(IL + IR) (IL and IR are the emission intensities of corresponding left and right circularly polarized light), the largest |glum| value is equal to 2. However, for most organic CPL systems tested in solution, the glum value is generally between 10−5 − 10−3, which is far less than the theoretical maximum value [6–11].

For enlarging the CPL glum value, many methods have been developed, such as inducing chiral nematic liquid crystal, mimicking hierarchical chirality transfer, constructing symmetry breaking, co-assembling AIE dye with helical nano- and microfilaments, employing surface-modified cellulose nanocrystals and adjusting chiral molecular conformation of bent-core molecules, etc [12–17]. Among these approaches, supramolecular self-assembly has been regarded as an effective way in recent years. In general, self-assembly realizes chirality transfer between chiral hosts and achiral fluorescent guests through the co-assembly process in which molecules or other assembly units spontaneously form ordered structures, endowing the co-assembly materials both large glum and high QY [7].

Till now, multiple chiral hosts have been used for CPL generation, such as proteins, gels, nematic liquid crystals, aggregate induced luminescence (AIE) materials et al. [18–21]. At present, the exploration of biocompatible templates for CPL emission has become an attractive area of research [22]. For example, double-strand DNA has been reported to co-assemble with achiral carbazole-based fluorophores for CPL-active design [23]. Simultaneously, our group reported that G-quadruplex DNA is an effective template for CPL emission, and its CPL property is completely determined by the G-quadruplex structure [24].

G-quadruplex is one of the most flexible DNA structures, and its basic structural unit is G-quartet with a cyclic planar tetramer, which is typically formed by the self-assembly of four guanosine residues [25]. G-quartet has also been considered as a powerful chiral template to design functional nanostructures such as hydrogels and nanofibers, attracting extensive attention in structural biology and supramolecular chemistry [26–28]. And among various G-quartet nanomaterials, G-quartet hydrogels mixed with functional substances such as fluorophores or drugs have been favored by researchers due to their excellent properties recently [29,30]. However, compared with the classical fluorophores, carbon dots (CDs)—a type of discrete spherical-like nanoparticle with a dimension lower than 10 nm, not only maintain the low toxicity and good biocompatibility of carbon materials, but also have good light stability, easy functionalization, low price, and easy large-scale synthesis [31].

Nowadays, CDs have shown promising applications in light-emitting devices, bioimaging and biodetection, catalysis, functional materials, due to their biocompatibility and exciting optical properties [32]. Especially in recent years, CDs have been served as preferred luminescent counterparts to co-assemble with various chiral template hosts such as cellulose nanocrystals, chiral glutamate and phenylalanine-derived gelators and the formed composite materials exhibit superb CPL properties and have been applied in optoelectronic devices and optical imaging, etc [33–39]. However, the above CDs-composited CPL-active materials all require complex synthesis steps and lengthy assembly process. More importantly, once assembled, the chiral orientation of the composite is completely dependent on the chirality of the template and cannot be flexibly switched. These factors greatly limit the wider application of CDs-composited materials in the field of CPL. Furthermore, for the self-assembly of CDs and G-quartet hydrogels, its chiral structure and CPL properties are still unspecified and ambiguous [40].

Herein, as shown in Scheme 1 , without any tedious preparation and compounding, the CDs-composited fluorescent G-quartet hydrogels were directly synthesized by the simple one-step microwave irradiation based on Guanosine 5′-monophosphate (GMP) as precursors. The obtained composite hydrogels can nimbly reverse and convert between left-handed and right-handed helical chirality by adding metal ions—K+ and tuning the kinetic formation process, and emit CPL corresponding to the chirality. Specifically, slow gelation process is conducive to the right-helix G-quartet stacking structure, while rapid gelatinization and the addition of K+ benefits the left-helix. The CPL glum ranged from −0.12 to 0.04, and composite hydrogels showed good thermal cycling stability and application potential in anti-counterfeiting printing.

Guanosine 5′-monophosphate disodium salt hydrate (GMP, ≥99%) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). KCl were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). All the chemicals were used without further treatment. All solutions were prepared using ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) from a Milli-Q automatic ultrapure water system.

GMP was dissolved in ultrapure water (4 mL) to prepare a high concentration stock solution of GMP (500 mM). Then 0.04 mL of the GMP stock solution was added to 0.96 mL of ultrapure water and diluted to a concentration of 20 mM, the diluted solution was put into a microwave reactor and the reaction conditions were 170 °C for 8 min. A viscous mild yellow clear liquid was obtained after completion of the reaction. The reacted solution was placed in a 1 KDa dialysis bag for three days and the water was changed every half a day to obtain purified GMP-CDs.

KCl was dissolved in ultrapure water (1 mL) to prepare a high concentration stock solution of KCl (1 M). Then 0.2 mL of the GMP stock solution and different volumes of KCl stock solution were dissolved in ultrapure water to make a total volume of 1 mL to prepare a mixed solution of GMP (100 mM) containing K+ (0–20 mM). The mixed solution was transferred to a microwave tube and put into a microwave reactor and after optimizing the experimental procedures, 170 °C and 8 min was chosen for the optimal reaction time and reaction temperature (Figs. S5–S7). After cooling to room temperature, the GMP-CDs-gel and GMP-CDs/K+-gel was directly obtained and the chirality of the hydrogel depends on the speed of gelling process or the addition of K+. (GMP-CDs/K+-gel with different concentrations of K+ only need to change the added concentration of K+).

The microwave reactor used the automated microwave digestion system (CEM Discover SP) manufactured by CEM corporation (North Carolina, USA), and the volume of the microwave tube is 10 mL. The power and frequency used for the microwave reaction are 150 W and 2450 MHz respectively. Microwave reaction time and temperature vary depending on conditions.

UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained at room temperature on a Lambda 750 spectrophotometer manufactured by Perkin-Elmer corporation (Massachusetts, USA) using a quartz cuvette with 1.0 cm path length. Spectra were scanned from 200 to 800 nm with a step size of 1 nm at a scan speed of 100 nm/min, and the bandwidths for incident light beam is 2 nm. All UV–Vis absorption spectra were tested at least three times and were repeatable.

XPS analysis were carried out at room temperature on a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD instrument manufactured by KRATOS corporation (Manchester, UK). All XPS spectroscopy were tested at least three times and were repeatable.

FTIR analysis were carried out at room temperature on a Nicolet iS50 FT-IR spectrometer manufactured by Thermo Fisher Scientific Corporation (Massachusetts, USA). Test samples were prepared by KBr tableting method and the spectrum was collected with 1 cm-1 resolution and 512 numbers of scan conditions in the range of 400–2000 nm. All FT-IR spectra were tested at least three times and were repeatable.

XRD analysis were carried out at room temperature on a D2 PHASE X-ray diffraction system manufactured by Bruker AXS corporation (Karlsruhe, Germany). Samples of gels were all freeze-dried before XRD analysis and the XRD patterns were measured in 2θ range from 10 to 80° with a step size of Δθ = 0.1° (Cu–Kα radiation, 40 kV, 40 mA, λ = 1.5418 Å). All XRD patterns were measured at least three times and were repeatable.

SEM images were conducted at room temperature on a GEMINI 300 field emission scanning electron microscope with SE2 detector manufactured by Zeiss corporation (Oberkochen, Germany). All samples of gels were evenly spread on the conductive adhesive to form a thin layer and left to dry before scanning. All SEM images were measured at least three times and were repeatable.

TEM images were conducted at room temperature on a Tecnai G2 F20 S-Twin TMP electron microscope manufactured by FEI corporation (Oregon, USA) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The GMP-CDs solution is dripped on the copper mesh to dry naturally before testing. All TEM images were measured at least three times and were repeatable.

Fluorescence spectra were obtained at room temperature on a FS5 spectrofluorometer manufactured by Edinburgh instruments corporation (Edinburgh, UK) using a quartz cuvette with 1.0 cm path length. Both excitation and emission scanning slits are 1 nm and dwell time is 0.3s. And the step size of both excitation and emission spectra were 1 nm. All fluorescence spectra were measured at least three times and were repeatable.

CD spectra were measured at room temperature on a J-1500 Circular Dichroism Spectrometer attached with a temperature controller manufactured by JASCO corporation (Tokyo, Japan) using a quartz cuvette with 1.0 mm path length. Samples were sandwiched between two 1 mm quartz plates. Spectra were scanned from 200 to 800 nm with a data pitch of 0.2 nm at a scan speed of 200 nm/min, and the bandwidth for incident light beam is 5 nm and D.I.T. is 2 s. All CD spectra were measured at least three times and were repeatable.

CPL spectra were measured at room temperature on a CPL-300 spectrometer attached with a temperature controller manufactured by JASCO corporation (Tokyo, Japan) using a quartz cuvette with 1.0 mm path length. Samples were sandwiched between two 1 mm quartz plates. The measure range of CPL spectrum is 390–550 nm with a data pitch of 0.2 nm and an excitation wavelength at 350 nm at a scan speed of 100 nm/min, and the bandwidths for incident and emission light beam are both 16 nm and D.I.T. is 4 s. Each CPL spectrum was an average of three measurements and were repeatable.

Fluorescence microscopy images were taken at room temperature on a FV3000 microscope manufactured by Olympus corporation (Tokyo, Japan). Samples are uniformly dispersed on glass slides for imaging observation. Fluorescence imaging was performed at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm.

The bright blue emitting CDs (GMP-CDs) were prepared by microwave irradiation of Na2 (5′-GMP) solutions according to Dash's work [40]. The morphology of GMP-CDs was confirmed by transmission electron microscope (TEM), size distribution and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) images showed the average diameter of GMP-CDs was less than 5 nm and without any graphitized lattice, proving that GMP-CDs were highly amorphous (Fig. 1A and S1A). The UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence (FL) studies were examined to evaluate the optical properties of GMP-CDs. As shown in Fig. S2, the UV–Vis spectrum of GMP-CDs has two peaks at 200 nm and 256 nm due to the π-π* transition of the guanine ring and the n-π* transition of the carbonyl group. And compared to GMP, the weak shoulder of GMP-CDs at 345 nm is attributed to the π-π* conjugation of the G-quartet's annular plane. FL spectroscopy of GMP-CDs showed that the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths are at 353 nm and 450 nm, respectively. And like any other CDs, GMP-CDs exhibited an excitation-dependent PL behavior [41–44]. As the excitation wavelength increased from 350 nm to 400 nm, the PL peaks of GMP-CDs shifted from 450 nm to 465 nm (Fig. 1 B). Additionally, the FL intensity of GMP-CDs almost remained stable even if irradiating continuously for 1 h under a 150 W Xe lamp, placing for one week, within the pH range of 4–10 and under the concentration of NaCl reached 0.5 M, indicating its excellent photostability [45] (Fig. S3).

The surface information of GMP-CDs was further analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) as shown in Fig. S4. The full XPS spectrum has four peaks at 286.25, 532.02, 399.10 and 132.80 eV, indicating that GMP-CDs are composed of C, N, O and P. Three distinct peaks at 284.80, 286.40 and 287.99 eV can be observed, corresponding to CC, C–O and CO/CN, respectively. The N 1s band has two peaks at 398.97 eV and 400.44 eV, which are attributed to CN and amino nitrogen, respectively. For the O 1s band, three peaks at 530.66, 532.54 and 535.87 eV are assigned to P–O, CO and C–OH, respectively. In addition, the P 2p band contains two peaks at 132.69 and 133.62 eV, which can be identified as P 2p3/2 and P 2p1/2 of phosphate group [46]. The above spectroscopic characterization data is consistent with the reference, indicating that GMP-CDs are indeed composed of GMP oligomers. And it has been confirmed that guanine and sugar component do not undergo rearrangement during the microwave treatment so that the resulting GMP-CDs can take advantage of the inherent self-assembly property of guanosine to structure fluorescent G-quartet hydrogels upon cooling to room temperature without addition of any external agents [40].

When continuing to explore the properties of CDs-composited G-quartet structured hydrogels. To our surprise, it can emit different degrees of CPL signal with the change of reaction conditions, which aroused our great interest in its chiral structure and CPL performance. Finally, 100 mM GMP, 170 °C and 8 min were selected as the best preparation condition by optimizing the experimental procedures (Figs. S5–S7), and the obtained co-assembly of GMP-CDs and hydrogels is abbreviated as GMP-CDs-gel for convenience.

The nanostructure of composite hydrogels was investigated in detail by SEM. For GMP-CDs-gel, it exhibits a uniform-sized fibrillar morphology with a distinct right-handed helical structure with a pitch of about 50 nm and tightly intertwined (Fig. 1C and S8A). Furthermore, inspired by our previous work that K+ can change the helical chirality of Sr2+-stabilized nanofibers and successfully achieve the chiral inversion of G-quartet structured nanofibers [47]. Under the same conditions, K+ (20 mM) was tried to be introduced to form G-quartet hydrogel (GMP-CDs/K+-gel). Interestingly, it can be clearly observed from Fig. 1D and S8B that GMP-CDs/K+-gel is more compactly aligned than GMP-CDs-gel, and its fibrillar nanostructure is denser. GMP-CDs/K+-gel has a left-handed helical structure with a pitch of about 30 nm, which further confirms that K+ are favorable for the formation of a left-handed G-quartet structure. In addition, after hydrogel was heated and melted, it changed back to a high-concentration GMP-CDs solution. After diluting it, the morphology of GMP-CDs was photographed by HRTEM again. It was found that there was no alteration, which proved the outstanding stability of the GMP-CDs (S1B).

In order to further prove that the basic composition structure of two composite hydrogels is G-quartet structure, their XRD and FT-IR spectra were measured respectively. As shown in Fig. S9, both hydrogels had a distinct peak at 27.4°, which could be related to the distance between adjacent vertical G-quartet, indicating a characteristic distance of 0.33 nm for stacking between G-quartets and demonstrating the G-quartet structure of composite hydrogels [40]. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2 A) uncovered that the surface of both hydrogels contained amide carbonyl (1695 cm−1), N–H (1524 cm−1), C–N (1482 cm−1), PO (1086 cm−1), C–H (1365 cm−1) [40]. Besides, when composite hydrogels are formed, the position of the GMP C-6=O stretching vibration band moved from 1675 cm−1–1695 cm−1, and the stretching and bending bands of the imidazole ring N-7-C-8 and C-8-H at 1493 cm−1 shifted to low wavenumbers, which proved that G-quartet was formed by hydrogen bonding of guanine unit [48]. The almost consistent infrared spectra of the two composite hydrogels also mean that they are bonded in the same way.

The structure and chirality of GMP-CDs-gel and GMP-CDs/K+-gel were further characterized by circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy (Fig. 2B). The CD spectra of the two CDs-composited G-quartet hydrogels exhibited symmetrical chirality, implying that their helical structures are opposite. For GMP-CDs-gel, the negative band of 260 nm and the shoulder peak of 290 nm are typical CD peaks of G-quartet structure, further indicating that the formed composite hydrogels are indeed a nanomaterial with G-quartet as its basic structural unit [48]. And the transitions and differences in CD signals for GMP-CDs/K+-gel are also consistent with the previously reported conclusion that K+ is beneficial to the formation of left-helix G-quartet structure [47]. Additionally, The CD spectrum of the dispersed and diluted GMP-CDs shows features similar to GMP (Fig. S10A), again indicating that GMP-CDs are composed of GMP oligomers and the presence of their guanine motifs would facilitate the self-assembly of GMP-CDs to form the extended G-quartet structure. The GMP solution without microwave treatment is also tested for CD spectra as a contrasted experiment (Fig. S10B), it can be seen that the gradual addition of K+ does not lead to the constitution of hydrogels, its CD signal and UV absorption do not change, and still maintain the classic weak positive CD cotton effect at 220 nm and absorption peak of the GMP monomer at 250 nm, respectively [48]. This also bear out that the GMP-CDs synthesized by microwave are indispensable for the formation of CDs-composited G-quartet structured hydrogels.

Since the prepared CDs-composited G-quartet hydrogels exhibited excellent fluorescence and chirality property, its CPL property was measured (Fig. 2C). Interestingly, GMP-CDs-gel showed right-handed CPL (R-CPL) signal ranging from 400 to 550 nm with a peak at 440 nm. Subsequently, the effect of K+ on the CPL signal was investigated. The intensity of R-CPL signal emitted by the GMP-CDs-gel decreased gradually and finally became left-handed CPL (L-CPL) as the K+ concentration gradually increased. Fig. 2D plotted the corresponding glum values of GMP-CDs/K+-gel under different K+ conditions, ranging from 0.02 to 0.04, indicating that GMP-CDs-gel had a high controllable CPL dissymmetry factor and can be regulated by K+. In addition, without any further chemical modifications and tedious post-processing procedures, the absolute quantum yield (QY) of GMP-CDs-gel was determined to be 34.6% at 353 nm excitation wavelength, which was slightly lower than 35.5% of GMP-CDs using a quantum efficiency measurement system with a calibrated integrating sphere, proving its superior CPL performance (Fig. S11).

Then, the melting temperature of CDs-composited G-quartet hydrogels was studied. The composite hydrogels gradually disintegrate into a viscous molten yellow-brown liquid with increasing temperature, and their CPL signal gradually approaches zero (Fig. 3 A). From the melting temperature plots of GMP-CDs-gel (Fig. 3B), it can be seen that when the temperature is higher than 22 °C, the R-CPL signal gradually decreases and the gel begins to melt. When the temperature rises to 50 °C, the signal approaches zero, indicating that the gel structure is completely decomposed at this temperature, and its melting temperature is 35 °C. For the GMP-CDs/K+-gel, its initial dissociation temperature and melting temperature are 35 °C and 50 °C, respectively, indicating that the G-quartet hydrogels structure containing K+ is more stable and has better high temperature resistance. This may be that the addition of K+ can promote the self-assembly of GMP, further stabilize the G-quartet structure in the system [49]. We further tested the stability of GMP-CDs-gel and GMP-CDs/K+-gel by measuring the CPL recovery strength under cyclic heat treatment (Fig. 3C and D). During the four cycles of temperature increase/decrease, both hydrogels showed excellent recovery.

Metal cations, such as K+ and Na+, are generally considered to combine with the four oxygen atoms in the center of the G-quartet, acting as a stabilizer to strengthen G-quartet structure [28]. Moreover, the two previous works of our group reported that K+ can not only change the glycosidic angle of the G-quartet to induce the inversion of the stacking polarity of Sr2+-stabilized G-quartet nanofibers to left-handed, but also accelerate the formation of Sr2+-stabilized G-quartet nanofibers, leading to the synthesis of left-handed G-quartet nanostructures through ordered stacking during a faster reaction process [47,49]. For further explore the effect of K+ on GMP-CDs-gel, we first heated the microwave-synthesized gel to a molten state, then added different concentrations of K+ and made gel was under the same conditions, and compared the effect of K+ addition on composite hydrogels before and after the microwave process (Fig. S12). With the K+ concentration increases, the CPL signal of the latter also changes from negative to positive gradually. However, unlike adding K+ before microwave irradiation, more K+ is required after irradiation to achieve the same intensity of induced L-CPL. This may be due to the addition of K+ before microwave promotes the formation of G-quartet during the formation of composite hydrogels on the one hand, and changes the glycosidic angle of the G-quartet hydrogels on the other hand, while the addition of K+ after microwave only changes the glycosidic angle, so more K+ is required to obtain the same CPL signal value.

In order to further explore the factors that influence the helix chirality of CDs-composited G-quartet hydrogels, more studies were carried out. Since all previous CDs-composited G-quartet hydrogels were synthesized under stirring conditions, we explore the effect of stirring on helix chirality, that is, stopping the agitation during microwave irradiation and the first gelation. To control variables, the GMP-CDs/K+-gel was synthesized under the same conditions without stirring and with controlled stirring rate, respectively. We could clearly see that the GMP-CDs/K+-gel formed in two ways exhibit mirrored helix chirality, and GMP-CDs/K+-gel formed without stirring shows the exactly opposite R-CPL as before (Fig. 4 A and B), which indicated that the gelation process has a decisive effect on the helical chirality of CDs-composited G-quartet hydrogels. In order to determine whether it is the chiral effect induced by vortex direction, clockwise and counterclockwise vortex are introduced into the gelation process. The results showed that the opposite direction of vortex had no effect on the chirality of composite hydrogels, indicating that vortex is not the most cardinal reason affecting their chiral structure.

Recently, kinetic controlled chiral supramolecular polymerization with helical inversion is under intense investigation [50]. Therefore, we speculate that the change of the kinetic process induced by vortex is the fundamental reason for the transform of the helical chirality of composite hydrogels. Similarly, the chirality of GMP-CDs/K+-gel formed at different annealing rates was studied to explore this reason. We are surprised to find that GMP-CDs/K+-gel with the same synthetic conditions vary greatly under different annealing rates. As shown in Fig. 4C, with the annealing rate changing from fast (immediately) to 1.0 °C/min (slow process), the CPL signals were reversed from L-CPL to R-CPL, indicating the basic reason affecting the helical chirality is the gelation rate. Most importantly, the glum values of GMP-CDs/K+-gel under different annealing rate changed from 0.005 to −0.12, showing that composite hydrogels have a high and controllable CPL dissymmetry factor (Fig. 4D and Fig. S13). In addition to temperature, initial reactant concentration was also investigated to further demonstrate the effect of gelation rate. Under the condition of constant annealing rate, the chirality of GMP-CDs/K+-gel was reversed from right-handed to left-handed with the increase of the initial reactant concentration (Fig. S14), which once again proved that the rapid gelation process is beneficial to the left-handed G-quartet chiral stacking, while the slow gelation process favours the right-handed G-quartet chiral stacking.

GMP-CDs-gel and GMP-CDs/K+-gel present a mirror image of CPL, stable fluorescence emission and excellent high temperature resistance, making them have the application potential in encrypted transmission, 3D printing and anti-counterfeiting printing [51]. As shown in Fig. S15, the two prepared composite hydrogels were observed in optical mode of the fluorescence microscope, showing tight arrangement, well-dispersion and blue fluorescence under laser excitation. Furthermore, inspired by the anti-counterfeiting ink, we coated the melted GMP-CDs-gel on the hollow plate with a customized pattern (Fig. 5 A). After GMP-CDs-gel was slowly cooled and solidified, hollow plate was carefully removed and the formed 3D pattern emitted bright and uniform blue fluorescence under a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp (Fig. 5B). Because of its R-CPL, professional instruments can be used to identify anti-counterfeiting patterns and realize the transmission of encrypted information.

In conclusion, we synthesized bright blue fluorescent emitting GMP-CDs by an easily one-step microwave method. And once GMP-CDs were generated, they utilized their retained self-assembly properties to constitute CDs-composited fluorescent G-quartet hydrogels without any cumbersome manipulation and assembly steps. In addition, the chiral structure and CPL property of composite hydrogels can be flexibly regulated by the K+ and formation kinetics. The slow gelatinization determined the formation of the right-helix CDs-composited G-quartet hydrogels, and fast gelatinization and the addition of K+ corresponded to the left-helix. Finally, the CDs-composited G-quartet hydrogels are used as anti-counterfeit printing for information encryption, and they possessed excellent thermal cycling stability and a glum value as high as 10−1, revealing their great potential in CDs related CPL materials design and application.

Zhiwei Zhang: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Jingqi Chen: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Xuetao Yan: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis. Xiaowei Liu: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis. Yingying Chen: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Chuanqi Zhao: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Lingyan Feng: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [No. 22177067] and the National Natural Science Foundation of China [No. 22122704]; the Program for Professor of Special Appointment (Eastern Scholar) at Shanghai Institutions of Higher Learning [No. TP2016023]; the Shanghai Rising-Star Program [No. 20QA1403400].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2022.11.023.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoresolution of an axially chiral bicyclo[3.3.0]octan-3-one: phototriggers for a liquid-crystal-based optical switch
